topic.title_cn: 解释你如何做选择（权衡利弊）
topic.prompt_en: Explain how you usually make choices by weighing pros and cons.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to describe choice-making through criteria, trade-offs, and final judgment.

angles.title: Name the process
content: Start by saying what you are trying to achieve.
That gives the listener a reason to care about the steps.

angles.title: Break it into steps
content: Explain the process in a sequence people can picture.
Do not rush through the middle of the answer.

angles.title: Mention one difficulty
content: A real obstacle makes the explanation more useful and believable.
Show how you respond when the process is not smooth.

angles.title: Show your judgment
content: End with how you choose, prioritise, or adjust.
That is where your thinking becomes visible.

sequence.target: State what process, problem, or choice you are talking about.
Walk through the main steps and explain why they matter.
End with the principle or trade-off that guides you.

high_score_tips: Keep the explanation practical and linear so the listener can follow it easily.
Mention one real difficulty to avoid sounding like a textbook answer.
Use clear markers like first, after that, and in the end to control the flow.
Your final sentence should show what principle guides your decisions.

content.why_it_hurts: A process feels mechanical if the listener never hears why each step matters.
content.fix: Add a short reason or condition after the important steps.

content.why_it_hurts: Too much detail hides the main structure of the process.
content.fix: Focus on the steps that change the outcome or decision.

content.why_it_hurts: The answer stays generic if it never shows how you choose or adapt.
content.fix: End with the factor you care about most, such as time, quality, or risk.

content.why_it_hurts: The response sounds memorised if it stays at the level of general ideas and never lands on one lived detail.
content.fix: Add one concrete moment, reaction, or small consequence that actually happened to you.

content.problem: Reflection that only repeats the opening
content.why_it_hurts: The ending feels flat if it only restates the topic instead of adding insight or change.
content.fix: Use the final lines to show what changed, what you realised, or how you would respond now.

language.wrong_example: I solve this problem with checking everything again.
language.better_example: I solve this problem by checking everything again.
language.reason: By is the natural linker before a method or action.

language.wrong_example: This way is more easier for me.
language.better_example: This way is easier for me.
language.reason: Comparative adjectives do not take both more and -er.

language.problem: Overusing very or really with basic adjectives
language.wrong_example: It was very good and really important for me.
language.better_example: It turned out to be more meaningful than I expected.
language.reason: A precise evaluation sounds more natural than stacking intensifiers on basic adjectives.

language.problem: Repeating I think in every sentence
language.wrong_example: I think it is useful, and I think it matters because I think it affects daily life.
language.better_example: What makes it useful to me is the way it affects daily life.
language.reason: Removing repeated I think makes the answer sound more confident and more fluent.

core.example: I would naturally use "process" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "step" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "priority" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "option" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "decision" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "method" when talking about this topic.

extended.note: Useful when explaining complex work in smaller steps.
Good for practical solutions and realistic choices.
Useful for decision and priority answers.
Good for explaining how you judge choices.

core.example: I would use "the first step is" naturally in this answer.
I would use "after that" naturally in this answer.
I would use "what matters most is" naturally in this answer.
I would use "it depends on" naturally in this answer.

core.phrase: I usually work through it by
core.example: I would use "I usually work through it by" naturally in this answer.

core.phrase: there is always a trade-off between
core.example: I would use "there is always a trade-off between" naturally in this answer.

extended.example: I would use "the most workable option is" when making the answer sound more natural.
I would use "I narrow it down by" when making the answer sound more natural.
I would use "the bigger priority is" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: I try not to overcomplicate it
extended.example: I would use "I try not to overcomplicate it" when making the answer sound more natural.

framework.goal: Explain a process or decision through steps, difficulty, and judgment.

moves: Name the task, problem, or choice clearly.
Tell the listener what result you are trying to reach.
Walk through the main steps in order.
Add one challenge and how you handle it.
State the trade-off or priority behind your decision.
Keep the ending practical rather than abstract.

delivery_markers: The way I usually handle it is ...
In the end, I care most about ...

samples.answer:
The way i make choices when there is no perfect option is something I can talk about quite naturally. When I have to choose between two decent options, I normally start by asking what matters most in that situation. Sometimes that is time, sometimes it is long-term value, and sometimes it is simply which option will create less stress later. I usually write down the main pros and cons because vague thinking feels persuasive until I see it on paper. If both choices still look reasonable, I pay attention to the downside I can live with more easily.

What made it worth talking about was not only the event or idea itself, but also the effect it had on the way I think or act. That habit helps me make decisions without expecting a perfect answer every time. Overall, it is a topic that feels real to me because it connects to ordinary life rather than a perfect story.
highlights: Band 6 answer matched to the 解释你如何做选择（权衡利弊） topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

opening: The topic that comes to mind is the way I make choices when there is no perfect option.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: That habit helps me make decisions without expecting a perfect answer every time.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: I usually write down the main pros and cons because vague thinking feels persuasive until I see it on paper.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.original: It was very important to me.
upgrades.improved: What made it important was the effect it had on the way I handled similar situations later.
upgrades.why: This explains importance through a concrete effect instead of a vague adjective.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want a simple but natural reflective sentence.

upgrades.original: I learned a lot from it.
upgrades.improved: I came away from it with a clearer idea of what I would do next time.
upgrades.why: This sounds more spoken and more specific than a flat learned a lot.
upgrades.note: Good for endings that focus on takeaway and future behaviour.

samples.answer:
The topic that comes to mind for me is the way I make choices when there is no perfect option. When I have to choose between two decent options, I normally start by asking what matters most in that situation. Sometimes that is time, sometimes it is long-term value, and sometimes it is simply which option will create less stress later. What stood out most was that i usually write down the main pros and cons because vague thinking feels persuasive until I see it on paper.

After that, if both choices still look reasonable, I pay attention to the downside I can live with more easily. What I find interesting about it now is that the situation seemed fairly ordinary at the time, but it revealed something more lasting about how I handle pressure, choices, or relationships. That habit helps me make decisions without expecting a perfect answer every time. So if I were explaining this in a speaking test, I would focus less on sounding dramatic and more on why the experience or idea genuinely stayed with me.

Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

upgrades.improved: It gave me a more balanced way to look at similar situations afterwards.
upgrades.why: Balanced way to look at is a more idiomatic reflective phrase in spoken English.
upgrades.note: Works well in opinion, process, and experience answers.

samples.answer:
If I had to answer this topic in a more reflective way, I would still begin with the way I make choices when there is no perfect option. When I have to choose between two decent options, I normally start by asking what matters most in that situation. Sometimes that is time, sometimes it is long-term value, and sometimes it is simply which option will create less stress later. The turning point, for me, was that i usually write down the main pros and cons because vague thinking feels persuasive until I see it on paper.

From there, if both choices still look reasonable, I pay attention to the downside I can live with more easily. What matters now is not the surface event or statement alone, but the shift in perspective behind it. That habit helps me make decisions without expecting a perfect answer every time. In that sense, the topic feels useful because it shows not only what happened or what I think, but also how my judgment has become a little more precise over time.

Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

upgrades.note: Strong for higher-band closings that need subtlety rather than drama.

upgrades.improved: Its real value lay in the way it quietly reshaped my response patterns over time.
upgrades.why: Quietly reshaped and over time make the reflection sound more precise and more natural.

practice.task: Explain how you usually make choices by weighing pros and cons.
checklist: State the goal before the steps.
Use a clear sequence instead of loose ideas.
End with the judgment or trade-off that guides you.
self_prompts: Where does the main difficulty appear?
How do you decide what matters most?
What would make you change your approach?
